If someone wants to send exactly the \\\"Notes\\\" case study to a hiring manager, they shouldn't have to tell them to \\\"scroll down and click the first box\\\". Every piece of work should be deep-linkable!\\n\\nSince your portfolio is a beautifully continuous single-page scrolling experience, we don't want to break that flow by adding rigid, multi-page routing. \\n\\nInstead, I propose adding a **URL Query Parameter Sync**.\\n\\n## How it will work:\\n1. **Dynamic URLs:** When you open a case study, the URL in the browser will instantly update to `yoursite.com/?study=notes` (or `?study=salary-pages`). \\n2. **Copy & Share:** You can copy that exact URL and send it to anyone.\\n3. **Deep Linking:** When someone clicks that link, the portfolio will load normally, but it will instantly pop open the correct Case Study modal.\\n4. **Next/Previous Navigation:** As you click \\\"Next Case Study\\\" from within the modal, the URL will seamlessly update in the browser bar to match what you're reading.\\n5. **Native Back Button Support:** If you open a case study and then hit the browser's \\\"Back\\\" button, it will elegantly close the modal instead of throwing you off the website entirely.\\n\\n## Technical Approach\\nWe will keep this extremely lightweight so it doesn't break the single-page scroll layout:\\n- Update `CaseStudies.tsx` state to read from `window.location.search` on initial load.\\n- Hijack the `setActive` state updater to push state to the browser's History API (`window.history.pushState`).\\n- Add a `popstate` event listener so the modal responds instantly to the browser's forward/back arrows.\\n\\nDoes this lightweight query-parameter approach sound perfect for your needs?\"","Description":"\"Created an implementation plan for Shareable URLs.\"","IsArtifact":"true","Overwrite":"true","TargetFile":"\"/Users/anmol.maggon/.gemini/antigravity-ide/brain/c492a267-f4ae-42f9-a60d-1212cc4ca6f6/implementation_plan.md\"","toolAction":"\"Creating plan for URLs\"","toolSummary":"\"Plan shareable URLs\""}}]}

{"step_index":510,"source":"MODEL","type":"PLANNER_RESPONSE","status":"DONE","created_at":"2026-06-12T12:21:38Z","content":"Ah, that's a classic rendering bug in modern browsers! \n\nBecause your homepage has a highly interactive 3D WebGL background (like the floating hero objects or the tech stack jar), the browser's GPU sometimes struggles to correctly composite the heavy 3D canvas behind the scrolling DOM of the modal. When you scroll quickly, the browser tries to repaint the modal, and the 3D canvas briefly \"punches through\" the layers, causing that flicker.\n\nI just deployed a fix! I added a **hardware-acceleration flag** (`transform-gpu`) to the modal's background containers. This forces the browser to render the modal on its own dedicated GPU composite layer, completely isolating it from the 3D canvases underneath.\n\nRefresh the page and give the case study a hard scroll—it should be perfectly solid now with absolutely zero background bleed-through!"}

(b) ALWAYS use grep_search instead of running grep inside a bash command unless absolutely needed. (c) DO NOT use ls for listing, cat for viewing, grep for finding, sed for replacing.
